questions.question: Explain the three main steps of Divide and Conquer paradigm with an example.
keyPoints: DIVIDE: Break problem into smaller subproblems of same type
CONQUER: Recursively solve subproblems
COMBINE: Merge solutions of subproblems
Example: Merge Sort divides array, sorts halves, then merges

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for Merge Sort and solve it using Master Theorem.
keyPoints: Compare n^(log2 2) = n with f(n) = n
They are equal, so case 2 applies
Solution: T(n) = Θ(n log n)

questions.question: Explain why Quick Sort has O(n²) worst-case complexity and how it can be avoided.
keyPoints: Worst case when pivot is always smallest or largest element
Occurs in already sorted or reverse sorted arrays
Can be avoided by using randomized pivot selection
Or using Median of Medians for guaranteed O(n log n)

questions.question: Describe the Divide and Conquer approach for finding the Closest Pair of Points.
keyPoints: Sort points by x-coordinate
Recursively find closest pairs in left and right halves
Combine step: consider points within strip of width 2d
Sort strip by y-coordinate and check adjacent points

questions.question: What is the Master Theorem? Explain its three cases with examples.
keyPoints: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a≥1, b>1
Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)) → Θ(n^(log_b a))
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)) → Θ(n^(log_b a) log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) → Θ(f(n))

questions.question: Explain how FFT reduces complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n).
keyPoints: DFT of size n broken into two DFTs of size n/2
Even indices: X[2k] = DFT of even-indexed samples
Odd indices: X[2k+1] = DFT of odd-indexed samples
Combine using formula requiring O(n) time

questions.question: Compare Merge Sort and Quick Sort on the basis of time complexity, space complexity, and stability.
keyPoints: Time: Both O(n log n) average, Quick Sort O(n²) worst
Space: Merge Sort O(n), Quick Sort O(log n)
Stability: Merge Sort stable, Quick Sort unstable
Cache: Quick Sort better due to in-place partitioning

questions.question: Explain Strassen's Matrix Multiplication algorithm. Derive its time complexity and explain why it is better than naïve multiplication.
keyPoints: Strassen reduces 8 multiplications to 7 for 2×2 matrices
Defines 7 intermediate matrix computations (M1 to M7)
Recursively computes these submatrices
Time complexity: T(n) = 7T(n/2) + O(n²) = O(n^2.807)
Better than O(n³) naïve method for large matrices

questions.question: Explain the Median of Medians algorithm for finding the kth element in O(n) time. How is it used to guarantee O(n log n) worst-case for Quick Sort?
keyPoints: Divide n elements into ⌈n/5⌉ groups of 5
Find median of each group (constant time)
Recursively find median of these medians (⌈n/10⌉ elements)
Use this median as pivot for partition
Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/5) + T(7n/10) + O(n) = O(n)
Guarantes O(n log n) worst-case for Quick Sort when used as pivot selection

questions.question: Describe the Graham Scan algorithm for finding Convex Hull using Divide and Conquer approach. Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Sort points by x-coordinate (and y if tie)
Use stack to maintain hull vertices
Process points from left to right
Remove points that cause concave angle (non-counterclockwise)
Combine step merges hulls from divide phase
Time: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for scan = O(n log n)

questions.question: What are Cache-Oblivious algorithms? Explain how divide and conquer helps in designing efficient cache-oblivious algorithms.
keyPoints: Algorithms that work efficiently regardless of cache size
Do not require knowledge of cache parameters
Divide and conquer naturally has good spatial locality
Recursion ensures working set size decreases
Example: Cache-oblivious B-tree, Matrix multiplication
Achieve optimal transfer of blocks between cache levels
